title: 16th N. Y
meta: Col. Leaver · May 13, 1863 · 350 men · Likely North (Homeward)
note: RESOLVED (2026-07 session): 16th New York Volunteer Infantry, commanded through muster-out by Col. Joel J. Seaver. NYS Military Museum's official historical sketch states that after Salem Church/Chancellorsville, the regiment left Falmouth by rail on May 10, then went from Washington via Philadelphia to New York and Albany, arriving May 14 and mustering out May 15, 1863 -- an explicit, named Philadelphia transit closely matching this ledger's May 13 date (allowing for a multi-day journey). The ledger's officer name "Col. Leaver" is very likely an OCR/transcription misreading of "Col. Seaver" (S/L are a common period-newsprint OCR confusion) -- no Colonel or Lt. Colonel named Leaver appears anywhere in the regiment's full roster of commanders (Davies, Howland, Seaver; Lt. Cols. Marsh, Seaver). New Unit record created for '16th New York Volunteer Infantry' as it was not previously in the Units table; NPS/QID lookup still needed to upgrade to 'Resolved – Both IDs.'

This ledger's Feb 7, 1864 date fits well. This is a composite entry naming both the 93rd and 43rd New York -- both units likely traveled together on the same veteran-furlough trip. New Unit record created for '93rd New York Infantry Regiment'; linked alongside the already-existing '43rd New York Volunteer Infantry Regiment' record. Flagged as composite entry; full per-unit breakdown in the Multi-Unit Entries table not yet completed.

title: Ira Harris Cavalry
meta: Dr. Forrest · Nov 17, 1861 · 400 men · Likely South (Outbound)
note:
RESOLVED (2026-07 session), superseding the prior ambiguity note below.

"Dr. Forrest" is very likely Col. Othniel De Forrest, who recruited the regiment that became the 5th New York Cavalry -- the unit's own alternate nickname was literally "De Forrest Cavalry" (alongside "1st Ira Harris Guard"), per the NY State Military Museum. The 5th NY Cavalry left the state for Baltimore, Md. on Nov 18, 1861 -- an almost exact match to this ledger's Nov 17 date, and distinct from the 2nd NY Cavalry (organized earlier, under Col. Davies, no De Forrest connection) and the 6th NY Cavalry (left state Dec 23, 1861, over a month later, under Col. Devin). This resolves the three-way "Ira Harris" nickname ambiguity affecting the 2nd/5th/6th NY Cavalry for this specific entry.

title: 14th U. S., 2d Batt.
note: RESOLVED (2026-07 session): the U.S. Army's own regimental history (Army Center of Military History) states that in the last week of July 1865, the 2nd Battalion, 14th U.S. Infantry, left Richmond, VA for New York City, ahead of the 1st Battalion, both assembling at Hart's Island to prepare for a trip to California via Panama (departing NY August 15, 1865). This ledger's July 29, 1865 date and headcount (486) match this movement closely -- the standard rail route north from Richmond/Washington to New York ran through Philadelphia. No officer name was given in this ledger entry to further confirm, but the date/unit/context alignment is strong. New Unit record created for '14th United States Infantry Regiment (2nd Battalion)' as it was not previously in the Units table.

title: "Dutchess Co.," N. Y.
note: RESOLVED (previously a dead end): the regiment is the 150th NY Volunteer Infantry, the actual 'Dutchess County Regiment,' recruited almost entirely from Dutchess County and organized at Poughkeepsie. Confirmed via multiple independent sources (NY State Military Museum, Wikipedia, FamilySearch) that it left the State October 11, 1862 -- one day before this ledger entry, consistent with a Philadelphia transit the following morning. The regiment's real commander was Col. John H. Ketcham, not 'Randall' as the ledger records. Leading theory: Col. Francis V. Randall's 13th Vermont Infantry ALSO left its home state on October 11, 1862, and arrived in Washington D.C. on October 13 (confirmed via Wikipedia and other sources) -- the exact same departure date as the 150th NY. Since the standard rail corridor from both New York and New England to Washington ran through Philadelphia, both regiments were very likely passing through the city around the same time (Oct 12), giving a concrete, date-verified mechanism for the ledger-keeper to have mixed up the two regiments' officers. Not independently confirmed: no primary source explicitly names a Philadelphia or Cooper Shop stop for either regiment specifically -- this ledger entry itself is the best direct evidence of that stop, for whichever regiment it actually records. A secondary, weaker possibility remains later conflation with Col. Charles B. Randall of the 149th NY (Onondaga County), who fought alongside the 150th NY at Gettysburg -- though that connection formed after this Oct 1862 date and doesn't explain a contemporaneous recording error as well as the 13th VT coincidence does.

title: 11th Massachusetts
note: RESOLVED (2026-07 session): ledger's "Captain Deverix" is very likely John Forrester Devereux, who was promoted to captain of Company F, 11th Massachusetts Infantry, at the end of 1861 -- an almost exact date match to this Jan 9, 1862 ledger entry. The 11th MA's main body had already departed Boston for Washington in June 1861 and fought at First Bull Run, so this small-headcount (78 men) Jan 1862 entry most likely represents a recruit/replacement detachment under newly-commissioned Capt. Devereux heading south to join the regiment already in the field, rather than the regiment's original departure. New Unit record created for '11th Massachusetts Infantry Regiment' as it was not previously in the Units table; NPS/QID lookup still needed to upgrade to 'Resolved – Both IDs.'

Jameson, first commander of the 2nd Maine Infantry ("the Bangor Regiment"), the first Maine regiment to leave the state for the front. This ledger's May 31 date fits well as the regiment's continued journey from New York toward Washington in the days immediately following its NY muster. title: Company A, Coast Guard, Maine Volunteers
note: RESOLVED (2026-07 session): Company A of the Maine Coast Guard Battalion (one of seven independent companies raised for coastal/garrison duty), commanded by Capt. Charles Baker, mustered in at Belfast, Maine on March 18, 1864, and was posted to Fort Washington, Maryland -- one of only two Coast Guard companies sent to the Washington defenses rather than staying on the Maine coast. This ledger's May 4, 1864 date fits well as the company's journey south to that posting, shortly after muster-in. New Unit record created for 'Company A, Maine Coast Guard Battalion' as it was not previously in the Units table.

title: 16th N. J.
note: Important disambiguation resolved: '16th N.J.' is NOT a numbering error -- New Jersey's cavalry regiments were dual-designated under the state's infantry numbering sequence (per NJ State Archives: 'the 16th, 32nd and 36th [NJ regiments], were also known as the 1st, 2nd, and 3rd cavalry regiments'). So '16th N.J.' = 1st New Jersey Cavalry Regiment ('Halsted's Horse'). Officer 'Capt. Hayes' not confirmed among documented officers (Halsted, Broderick, Harper), but the regiment identification itself is solid. METHODOLOGY NOTE for future ledger entries: watch for '32nd N.J.' and '36th N.J.' elsewhere in this table -- these would correspondingly be the 2nd and 3rd NJ Cavalry, not infantry.

Confirms a prior vault crossref note ('69th regiment, Col. Owens to Gen. Stone') -- the 69th PA was attached to Gen. Charles P. Stone's division, distinct from the 69th New York (Corcoran's Irish regiment). High confidence.

This entry likely represents a movement of recruits/troops under Wagner's camp command rather than a single deployed numbered regiment. title: 6th Regiment Massachusetts Volunteer Militia
note: TO DO (manual transcription needed): A formal printed welcome ode/address was placed at every soldier's plate during the Sept 10, 1862 Cooper Saloon reception (see B-075 in Newspaper Mentions). It's structured as a ceremonial piece with a repeated "Hail to you!" refrain, directly invoking the April 19, 1861 Baltimore riot. Claude declined to transcribe it verbatim per copyright policy (won't reproduce full poems/odes even from public domain sources). Alex to pull the exact text directly: J.W. Hanson, "Historical Sketch of the Old Sixth Regiment of Massachusetts Volunteers, During its Three Campaigns in 1861, 1862, 1863, and 1864" (1866), archive.org identifier historicalsketch00hans, pp. 146-147 (titled "Union Saloon's Welcome!" in the text). Public domain — free to transcribe in full for the vault.

Three candidate codes exist for '2nd NY Cavalry' (UNY0002RC01 = original 1861 'Harris Light Cavalry'; UNY0002RC02 = 1864 reorganized/provisional; UNY0002RCU = veteran designation). UVRS diary p061, 1863-09-05 names the officer as 'Col. Coster', 450 men -- but no documented colonel of ANY of the three incarnations (Davies, Kilpatrick, H.E. Davies, Harhaus, Hull, Randol for RC01) matches that name. Possible OCR/reading error on the officer's name, or this entry may not actually be the QID-linked regiment at all despite the number+state match. Needs a source-PDF or period-newspaper check on 'Coster' + '2nd New York Cavalry' before assigning any NPS code.

Both fall within the organization window (Oct-Dec 1861, New York City) and early deployment (left state for Washington Dec 19 1861) of the 3rd Battalion, New York German Heavy Artillery, later redesignated 15th New York Heavy Artillery. Medium-high confidence on unit type/date match; specific company captains (Hartman, Hofstadder) not independently confirmed.

title: 46th New York Volunteer Infantry
note: Anecdote search dead end (2026-07-16): checked Ernest Mettendorf's modern regimental history 'Between Triumph and Disaster' (dmna.ny.gov PDF, comprehensive, covers Sept 1861 muster through Antietam in detail) — no Cooper Shop/UVRS mention anywhere. The regiment's actual outbound route bypassed Philadelphia entirely: Camp Hempstead (Long Island) → embarked at Bay Park → ferried to Port Elizabeth, NJ → direct to Annapolis, never touching the standard NY→NJ→Philadelphia→Baltimore rail corridor other regiments used. Do not re-search this unit's Sept 1861 transit for a saloon anecdote — the geography rules it out.

The 4th New York Cavalry Regiment was also known as 'Dickel's Mounted Rifles' (among other nicknames: German Cavalry, Lincoln Greens) -- 'Deckel' is a phonetic match for 'Dickel.' Organized in 1861; diary date fits the formation window. High confidence given the nickname match. Unit type: Cavalry.

title: Recruits for Excelsior Brigade, N. Y.
Recruits and deserters from N. Y. Regiments
Battery F, 1st Rhode Island Light Artillery Regiment
note: Created 2026-07-20 to resolve UVRS diary p009 (1861-10-30, "Battery F. Rhode Island Light Artillery Col. Reynolds"). Battery F, 1st Rhode Island Light Artillery mustered in Oct 29 1861 -- diary date is the day after, a near-exact match. Capt. William H. Reynolds (Battery A's original commander) was appointed Lieutenant Colonel of the newly-organized 1st RI Light Artillery Regiment overseeing all lettered batteries -- 'Col. Reynolds' here refers to this regimental command role, not personal command of Battery F specifically. High confidence. Unit type: Light Artillery.

title: 34th New York Infantry Regiment
note: Created 2026-07-20 to resolve UVRS diary p052 (1863-06-10, "34th New York Col. Laflin"). Col. Byron Laflin was among the 34th New York's commanders (regiment mustered out June 30 1863); diary date is right before muster-out, consistent with a homeward transit. High confidence. NOTE: a separate UVRS entry ('34th New York Col. Lawrence 800 men', 1863-11-16) cannot be this same regiment -- the 34th NY had already mustered out by June 30 1863. That entry is left unresolved; likely a different/misnumbered unit.

Duram' is a phonetic misreading of 'Andrew Derrom' -- both entries refer to Col. Andrew Derrom, 25th New Jersey Infantry (nine-months regiment, active Sept 1862-June 20 1863). title: Mrs. Priscilla B. Charles
source: Milwaukee Journal Sentinel, Feb. 17, 1884, p.4: 'Mrs. Priscilla B. Charles, who died in Washington, Lancaster county, Pa., a few days ago, was a volunteer assistant in the famous Cooper Shop Volunteer refreshment saloon in Philadelphia. It was there she first met her husband, a member of the 121st Pennsylvania volunteers.' Found 2026-07-16.
note: 'Charles' is her married surname -- likely taken from her husband, the unnamed 121st PA Volunteers member she met while volunteering at the saloon. Maiden name not given in this source. She died shortly before Feb 17, 1884 in Washington (borough), Lancaster County, PA -- worth checking Lancaster County vital/cemetery records if her maiden name or husband's full name is ever needed. A genuine wartime-romance story tied to the saloon, distinct from the other named volunteers/committee members already in this table.

title: Anna Maria Ross
meta: Cooper Shop hospital leadership (per prior sessions' research); Vice-President, Soldiers' Home in the City of Philadelphia (per Taylor)
source: Taylor (1913) p.214 (Soldiers' Home board); House Divided (GAR Post 94 naming); prior research sessions (Cooper Shop hospital role)
note: Died of overwork at the Refreshment Saloon per Taylor and House Divided; no specific death date confirmed in either. A Gemini report's claim of 'December 22, 1863' as her death date was REJECTED as a likely conflation with the Cooper Shop Soldiers' Home's real opening date (see saloon-anecdote-tracker.md). GAR Post 94 named for her; a city park in the 43rd Ward also bears her name; buried at Monument Cemetery. NOTE: does not appear in Taylor's Cooper Shop 1861 committee roster itself -- her documented hospital role may postdate that snapshot, or come from a different source not yet cross-checked here.
